does: This paper investigates personality measurement in large language models by comparing self-reported traits against actual behavioral outcomes across three dimensions: how personality profiles emerge and evolve during training stages, whether self-reported traits predict behavior in downstream tasks, and how interventions like persona injection affect both self-reports and behavior. The study systematically characterizes personality across models at different training stages and identifies a critical dissociation: while instruction tuning and RLHF stabilize trait expression and increase trait correlations in ways that resemble human personality patterns, these self-reported traits fail to reliably predict actual model behavior in behavioral tasks.
connects: This work builds on recent research treating LLMs as systems with measurable personality-like properties, extending beyond earlier simplified self-report approaches that relied on single-prompt elicitation and heuristic scoring. It directly responds to gaps in behavioral validation that plagued prior personality assessments of AI systems. The paper connects to the broader literature on mechanistic interpretability and model internals by investigating how training procedures like RLHF shape trait expression, and relates to work on prompt injection and persona engineering showing how external interventions modify model outputs. It also engages with psychological science examining trait validity and behavioral prediction in humans, applying these validation frameworks to artificial systems.
enables: These findings open new directions for understanding the gap between LLM outputs and underlying capabilities or values. By demonstrating that self-reported personality does not predict behavior, the work suggests that relying on LLM self-descriptions for alignment or safety purposes may be unreliable. Future research could investigate whether behavioral traits are more fundamental than self-reports, develop better methods for measuring actual model tendencies, and explore why instruction-tuning creates this illusion of personality without behavioral grounding. The dissociation also raises questions about whether behavioral consistency reflects genuine preferences or merely statistical patterns, with implications for interpretability and for designing more honest evaluation frameworks for LLM properties.
